data.title: Meta Platforms raises top executive pay with stock options amid intensifying AI race
data.description:
Meta Platforms (META) is granting stock options to its top executives for the first time since its 2012 IPO, aiming to retain and reward leadership as it continues heavy spending to stay competitive in the intensifying AI race.

The new options, outlined in company filings on Tuesday, will be released if Meta hits ambitious stock-price milestones over the coming years.

CFO Susan Li, CTO Andrew Bosworth, Chief product officer Chris Cox, COO Javier Olivan, president Dina Powell McCormick, and chief legal officer Curtis Mahoney are eligible for the stock options, regulatory filings showed. CEO Mark Zuckerberg, with a net worth of over $200 billion, is not part of the plan.

All executives except McCormick and Mahoney, who joined Meta (META) in January, will also receive an increase in restricted stock awards, worth a total of $170 million at last close, which vest quarterly. Chief Accounting Officer Aaron Anderson will only receive restricted stock.

For the first tranche of options to be paid out, Meta’s stock has to hit $1,116.08, an 88% increase from Tuesday’s closing price and equal to a market cap of about $2.82 trillion, based on current shares outstanding.

The next tranche requires a stock price of $1,393.87. The price goes up significantly for each additional tranche, with the highest being $3,727.12, which would make the company worth over $9 trillion. The most valuable company in the world today is Nvidia (NVDA).

The Facebook parent's stock price is down about 4% in the past year, trailing all of its megacap tech peers apart from Microsoft (MSFT), which has dropped 5%. Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), meanwhile, has jumped 73%, boosted by the success of its Gemini AI portfolio.

The company has also offered huge pay packages, some worth hundreds of millions ​of dollars over the years, to court top AI researchers to a new superintelligence team.

data.title: Merck close to $6B acquisition of Terns Pharma to boost cancer portfolio: FT
data.description:
Merck & Co. (MRK) is close to an approximately $6 billion all-cash deal to buy US biotech firm Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN) as it seeks to boost its drug pipeline ahead of cancer treatment Keytruda’s patent expiry, the Financial Times reported.

The talks are at an advanced stage and could be finalized in the coming days, the FT reported, citing people it didn’t identify. Terns (TERN) had a $5.4 billion market capitalization as of Tuesday’s close.

Merck and Terns didn’t immediately respond to requests for comment from the FT.

Following ⁠the news, shares of Terns Pharma, which focuses on developing treatments for ⁠chronic ​myeloid leukemia, rose about ​10% in after-hours trading.

Merck (MRK) has been pursuing deals to strengthen its pipeline, with CEO Rob Davis spearheading a strategy to diversify the company's portfolio ahead of the 2028 patent expiration for its blockbuster cancer drug, Keytruda.

data.title: Wednesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
7:00 AM MBA Mortgage Applications

The Mortgage Bankers' Association compiles various mortgage loan indexes.  The purchase applications index measures applications at mortgage lenders.

8:30 AM Import and Export Prices

Import price indexes are compiled for the prices of goods that are bought in the United States but produced abroad, and export price indexes are compiled for the prices of goods sold abroad but produced domestically

8:30 AM Current Account 

The current account, on a quarterly basis, measures the U.S. international balance in goods and services trade as well as unilateral transfers. 

8:30 AM Retail Inventories (Advance)

Retail inventories measure the monthly dollar value of inventories held by retailers.

8:30 AM Wholesale Inventories (Advance)

Wholesale inventories measure the monthly dollar value of inventories held by merchant wholesalers and are tracked to gauge inventory change in quarterly GDP.

10:30 AM EIA Petroleum Status Report

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on petroleum inventories in the U.S., whether produced here or abroad.

11:00 AM Survey of Business Uncertainty

The survey of business uncertainty is a panel survey measuring year-ahead expectations that US firms have about their own sales and employment growth.

11:30 AM 2-Yr FRN Note Auction

An FRN (floating rate note) is a security that has an interest payment that can change over time.

1:00 PM 5-Yr Note Auction

Treasury notes are sold at regularly scheduled public auctions. The competitive bids at these auctions determine the interest rate paid on each Treasury note issue.

4:10 PM Stephen Miran Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Stephen Miran participates in conversation before the Digital Asset Summit 2026.

data.title: Kb Home outlines shift to 70% built-to-order deliveries for stronger H2 2026 margins amid market uncertainty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KB Home (KBH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Jeffrey Mezger, Executive Chairman, indicated that first quarter results were within guidance and highlighted "our highest community count in many years, contributing to year-over-year growth in net orders." He emphasized a return to a "mix of sales that are predominantly built-to-order," aiming for "70% built-to-order deliveries in the second half of this year." Mezger noted repurchasing 843,000 shares and returning almost $70 million to shareholders in the quarter.
  • Mezger announced Rob McGibney's promotion to CEO and President, stating, "Rob is a proven results-oriented leader with a deep understanding of our business gained over the past 25 years with the company."
  • Rob McGibney, CEO and President, explained, "The meaningful improvement in cancellations reflects high-quality committed buyers who are ready and able to purchase a home and also supported net orders at an average absorption pace of 3.5 per month per community." He described the shift to the built-to-order (BTO) model as a "purposeful reset that positions us to be a stronger, more predictable company in the second half of the year and beyond."
  • McGibney added, "We are making considerable progress increasing our built-to-order sales. They represented 44% of our net orders in October, growing each month through the first quarter. We exited February at 68%. And in the early weeks of March, we are now above 70%."
  • Robert Dillard, CFO, stated, "In the first quarter of fiscal 2026, we were within our guidance range with total revenues of $1.08 billion and housing revenues of $1.07 billion, a 23% decrease on a year-over-year basis." Dillard added, "We continue to manage cost effectively and achieved an 8% reduction in total direct construction costs per unit."

Outlook

  • Dillard indicated, "In the second quarter of 2026, we expect to generate housing revenues between $1.05 billion and $1.15 billion, based on expected deliveries of between 2,250 and 2,450 homes. Housing gross profit margin, assuming no inventory-related charges, is expected to be between 15% and 15.6%."
  • For the full year 2026, KB Home expects "housing revenues of between $4.8 billion and $5.5 billion based on between 10,000 and 11,500 deliveries."
  • Management expects "margins to improve in the second half of 2026, driven largely by positive operating leverage from typical seasonality and a more favorable regional mix."
  • The company plans "between $50 million and $100 million of repurchases" for the second quarter and will provide margin guidance after the spring selling season.

Financial Results

  • KB Home reported total revenues of $1.08 billion and diluted earnings per share of $0.52 for the first quarter.
  • Delivered 2,370 homes in the quarter, near the midpoint of guidance.
  • Average selling price declined 10% to $452,000 due to regional and product mix.
  • Housing gross profit margin was 15.3%, with adjusted housing gross profit margin at 15.5% after excluding $2.2 million in inventory-related charges.
  • SG&A as a percent of housing revenue increased to 12.2%. SG&A expense decreased 14% due to lower unit volume and fixed cost controls, including a notable $8 million insurance recovery.
  • Net income was $33 million, with a 13% reduction in weighted average diluted shares outstanding.

Q&A

  • Matthew Bouley, Barclays: Asked about predictability benefits of the BTO model. Rob McGibney responded, "The benefits that it provides to the business in predictability. The first place I would go is that we've got this backlog of sold not started homes that we can leverage that gives us a cadence where we can operate on even flow production."
  • Bouley: Inquired about the guidance reduction drivers. McGibney explained, "Our orders while was a positive year-over-year comp... they were below our internal expectations... the last couple of weeks have been a little softer than what we would like to see... it's weighing on the consumer."
  • Matthew Johnson, UBS: Sought color on margin trajectory. Dillard clarified, "We don't expect meaningful changes in price, but we do expect to continue to get some delivery cost reductions... the shift of ETO should accumulate to an increase in gross profit margin."
  • Stephen Kim, Evercore ISI: Asked about backlog turnover ratio. McGibney said, "I think somewhere between that 60% number and the 80% number is probably where we'll fall."
  • Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates: Focused on pricing trends. McGibney noted, "About 70% of our communities during Q1 either had no change, or they had some level of small price increases... we did have still about 30% of our communities where we've moved price down further."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on guidance reductions, backlog predictability, and margin trajectory, with a slightly negative tone, frequently probing for clarity amid market uncertainty. Bouley and Ratner raised concerns about consumer demand and pricing stability.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational execution and the benefits of the BTO shift. In Q&A, McGibney acknowledged "noise out there" and uncertainty from geopolitical events, indicating a more cautious stance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management tone were more cautious, with greater emphasis on market unpredictability and guidance risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 was lowered, with full year housing revenue now expected between $4.8 billion and $5.5 billion and deliveries between 10,000 and 11,500, down from the previous guidance of $5.1 billion to $6.1 billion and 11,000 to 12,500 deliveries.
  • The company accelerated its transition to a "70% built-to-order" mix for the second half, up from 57% in Q4 2025.
  • Average selling price decreased further, and margins compressed more than anticipated in Q1, with management emphasizing cost controls and regional mix for improvement in H2.
  • Analysts shifted focus from general market dynamics to specific concerns on demand softness and the impact of geopolitical risks on consumer confidence.
  • Management's confidence in the BTO model and operational execution remained, but language reflected more caution around near-term market volatility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "affordability concerns and recent geopolitical tensions" as factors impacting guidance and near-term demand.
  • The conflict in the Middle East was specifically mentioned as introducing "another layer of uncertainty" affecting consumer confidence.
  • Material costs, particularly lumber, were highlighted as a source of cost pressure, though management is "managing our lumber locks strategically."
  • Management reduced headcount by 10% year-over-year to align overhead with lower delivery volumes.
  • Analysts raised concerns about sales pace, pricing stability, and the risk of higher mortgage rates affecting backlog conversions.

Final Takeaway

KB Home management emphasized a decisive shift back to its core built-to-order strategy, targeting at least 70% BTO deliveries in the second half of 2026 to drive higher margins and predictability. While first quarter results aligned with guidance, the company lowered its full year outlook due to softer than expected orders and heightened market uncertainty, particularly from geopolitical events. Management is focusing on cost discipline, operational efficiency, and a more favorable mix, with expectations of stronger performance and margin improvement in the latter half of the year as the benefits of the BTO transition and regional mix become more pronounced.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Absci outlines $25B annual market potential for ABS-201 in AGA and advances endometriosis clinical program
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Absci Corporation (ABSI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sean McClain reported "a strong fourth quarter," highlighting that ABS-201 is now in the clinic with the first three single ascending dose (SAD) cohorts dosed in the Phase I/IIa headline trial and "favorable emerging safety data." The company expanded into endometriosis as a second indication and published what it believes is the "first demonstration of de novo full-length antibody designed to zero-prior epitopes."
  • McClain introduced Dr. Ransi Somaratne, the new Chief Medical Officer, with registrational experience from Vertex. McClain noted, "Our development operations are in excellent hands because of the foundation [Andreas Busch] built."
  • McClain emphasized that ABS-201 was engineered for an extended half-life, with preclinical studies showing "a three to fourfold longer half-life than the competitor antibody," potentially enabling a "convenient dosing regimen of just 2 to 3 administrations for durable multiyear hair regrowth."
  • The company anticipates sharing "preliminary safety, tolerability and PK data for our ongoing headline trial in the first half of this year," with interim 13-week proof-of-concept data in the second half and full 26-week data in early 2027. ABS-201's safety, tolerability, and PK from the ongoing study will be used to support the initiation of the Phase II clinical trial in endometriosis in Q4 2026.
  • Chief Business Officer & CFO Zachariah Jonasson stated, "Revenue in the fourth quarter was $700,000 as we continue to progress our partnered programs." He added that research and development expenses rose to $25.3 million for the quarter, and the company ended the year with $144.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities.

Outlook

  • McClain stated, "In 2026, we expect to deliver on our catalysts, preliminary safety and PK data for ABS-201 in the first half, interim 13-week proof-of-concept hair regrowth data in the second half and initiation of our Phase II endometriosis trial in Q4, subject to data and regulatory review." The company also plans to share 26-week proof-of-concept data for ABS-201 and AGA in early 2027.
  • Jonasson reported that existing cash resources are expected to be sufficient to fund operations "into the first half of 2028."

Financial Results

  • The company reported $700,000 in revenue for the quarter. Research and development expenses were $25.3 million, while selling, general and administrative expenses were $8.6 million. A $5.1 million gain on the settlement of contingent consideration resulted in net proceeds of $8.7 million of unrestricted cash. Cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities as of December 31, 2025, totaled $144.3 million.
  • Jonasson indicated the increase in R&D expenses was "primarily driven by advancement of Absci's internal programs, including direct costs associated with external preclinical and clinical development of ABS-101 and ABS-201."

Q&A

  • Vamil Divan, Guggenheim Securities: Asked about the target product profile for ABS-201 and efficacy expectations. McClain indicated the goal is "a durable treatment as well as being able to achieve at or above minoxidil efficacy," and Jonasson elaborated, "if the effect size in terms of terminal area hair count and the growth in terminal area hair count is consistent with high-dose oral minoxidil, so 35 to 40 hairs per square centimeter, we think that's a home run product."
  • Divan also inquired about the safety profile. Somaratne responded, "there's no evidence of any on-target or off-target safety signal based on our review of the safety data accumulated to date, but it's encouraging so far."
  • Brendan Smith, TD Cowen: Asked about the 6-month primary endpoint for registrational studies and about drug creation partnerships. Somaratne noted, "other companies are developing a 6-month pivotal endpoint with another 6 months of long-term safety data follow-up...we're going to look forward to our 13-week interim to and give you more details on that once we see the data." Jonasson stated, "we continue to have productive discussions with pharma regarding platform partnerships."
  • Lut Ming Cheng, JPMorgan: Asked about partnering ABS-101, 301, and 501, and for more color on safety. McClain confirmed 301 and 501 are better suited for partners, and Somaratne said, "there's really nothing that would point to any sort of mechanism-related safety signal...but again, I have to caveat that it's early in the study."
  • Alexander Xenakis, Truist Securities: Asked about the ability of the molecule to engage the hair follicle target. McClain explained, "based on [animal data], we have no reason to believe you wouldn't be able to get an antibody into the follicle."
  • Debanjana Chatterjee, JonesTrading: Asked about positioning relative to new competitors. McClain described ABS-201 as "a brand-new novel mechanism," and Jonasson cited survey data showing high interest among current minoxidil users for ABS-201's potential profile.
  • Gil Blum, Needham: Asked about cohort dosing, endometriosis trial challenges, and resource allocation. Somaratne discussed the complexities of pain studies and trial design, and Jonasson emphasized both AGA and endometriosis provide "significant potential ROI," with streamlined development paths.
  • Sean Laaman, Morgan Stanley: Sought clarity on AI platform differentiation. McClain pointed to "the most rigorous work that has been published to date" and the application of agentic AI for asset development. Jonasson highlighted the higher value of asset deals compared to platform deals.
  • Charles Wallace, H.C. Wainwright: Inquired about market opportunity sizing for AGA and endometriosis. McClain responded, "AGA is likely a larger opportunity...these are both very exciting opportunities from just a market size perspective." Jonasson addressed pricing, stating, "we think the pricing for both of them...we don't think there'll be an arbitrage opportunity there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were pressing for clarity on efficacy targets, safety signals, competitive positioning, and commercial potential, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as management provided detailed responses and acknowledged early-stage limitations.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases like "we believe" and "we think that's a home run product," while repeatedly emphasizing the uniqueness and durability of ABS-201. Confidence was consistent with the previous quarter, though the addition of a new CMO brought further assurance regarding clinical execution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remained steady, focusing on program progress and risk management, while management's tone stayed confident and forward-looking.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw the addition of Dr. Ransi Somaratne as Chief Medical Officer and the transition of Andreas Busch to Co-Chair of the Scientific Advisory Board.
  • ABS-201's clinical timeline advanced, with the first SAD cohorts now dosed and favorable emerging safety data reported, compared to the previous quarter's focus on study initiation.
  • Management continued to prioritize ABS-201 in both AGA and endometriosis, with a more explicit emphasis on commercial strategy and market size in the latest call.
  • R&D expenses increased from $19.2 million in Q3 to $25.3 million in Q4, reflecting greater clinical activity.
  • Analysts continued to focus on efficacy endpoints, safety, market positioning, and partnership progress, with more attention this quarter on differentiation and the go-to-market approach.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged that ABS-201's clinical program is in early stages, repeatedly noting the need to "caveat that it's early in the study" and that safety data are "encouraging so far" but not yet comprehensive.
  • Somaratne highlighted that pain studies in endometriosis require careful site and patient selection and protocol design to mitigate placebo effects.
  • Questions from analysts touched on competitive threats from alternative therapies and the technological challenge of antibody tissue penetration.
  • Management cited ongoing engagement with the FDA and the dependence of future milestones on interim and final clinical data.

Final Takeaway

Absci closed 2025 with ABS-201 advancing in both androgenetic alopecia and endometriosis, reporting favorable emerging clinical safety and a robust cash position to support continued development. The company highlighted a potential $25 billion annual addressable market for ABS-201 in the U.S. alone and laid out a roadmap for key clinical readouts in 2026 and beyond. Management expressed confidence in the differentiated profile and commercial prospects of ABS-201 while reinforcing disciplined resource allocation and ongoing partnership discussions as central to its growth strategy.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BioCardia outlines imminent CardiAMP FDA submission and targets regulatory catalysts as trial data signals clinical benefit
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BioCardia (BCDA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Peter Altman, CEO, highlighted that BioCardia now has complete and final data from three clinical trials of CardiAMP cell therapy, with the latest Phase III results presented as a late-breaking clinical trial. He emphasized, "The key takeaway from these new results is in this title. The Cardiac HF echocardiography clinical results... show reductions in left ventricular volume disease when the heart ventricle is fully dilated with a p-value of 0.06 and when the heart is fully contracted with a p-value of 0.09." For the prespecified subgroups of patients with elevated biomarkers of heart stress, he noted differences were clinically meaningful and statistically significant.
  • Altman stated, "We expect to soon submit the Q-sub request on approvability of the CardiAMP system to FDA Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research, or CBER, based on the safety and compelling signals of patients benefits with elevated biomarkers of heart stress from our 3 clinical trials."
  • He also outlined four catalysts for the next quarter: the FDA CardiAMP Heart Failure Q-submission, a formal clinical consultation with Japan PMDA, an FDA feedback meeting on the Helix system, and an abstract presentation at EuroPCR in May.
  • David McClung, CFO, stated, "Total expense accretes approximately 3% year-over-year to $8.3 million in 2025 and compared to $8.1 million in 2024. The primary driver of this change, research and development expense, increased to $5 million in 2025 compared to $4.4 million in 2024."
  • McClung added, "Net loss increased modestly to $8.2 million in 2025 from $7.9 million in 2024. Net cash used in operations was approximately $7.5 million during the year into 2025. That's down from $7.9 million in 2024. The company ended the year with cash and cash equivalents totaling $2.5 million, very comparable to the $2.4 million as of December 31, 2024."

Outlook

  • Management expects to "soon submit the Q-sub request on approvability of the CardiAMP system to FDA Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research."
  • Altman expects a 45-day turnaround from FDA for Sprint discussion under breakthrough designation and notes, "If that's the door that's open and we decide to pursue it, it could be a very short time line... But if it's a PMA pathway... it could be a little longer."
  • R&D expenses are anticipated to increase modestly in 2026 as BioCardia continues to advance therapeutic candidates in the United States and Japan. SG&A expenses are expected to remain close to 2025 levels.

Financial Results

  • Total expenses for 2025 were reported as $8.3 million, with R&D expenses at $5 million and SG&A at $3.3 million. Net loss for 2025 was $8.2 million. Net cash used in operations was approximately $7.5 million, and year-end cash and equivalents totaled $2.5 million. Management expects cash burn in 2026 to remain consistent.

Q&A

  • Lander Egaña-Gorroño, H.C. Wainwright: Asked for color on the p-values for diastolic and systolic volumes and how the data supports the narrative to PMDA. Altman responded, "your question, Lander, was across all patients, the p-values are not statistically significant, but they're approaching it... our expectation is that our approvals both in the United States and in Japan will not be for the full cohort, but will be for the subgroup with elevated NT-proBNP."
  • Egaña-Gorroño queried about the CardiAMP submission timeline and post-marketing requirements. Altman stated, "We are -- as I shared in my remarks, we're imminently going to file for a discussion on approvable pathways... post-marketing studies, you cannot have patients come in and not have an option to therapy can't truly randomize. So we would expect these to be relatively extensive studies on many hundreds of patients that we would follow over time."
  • Egaña-Gorroño also asked about competition from other cell therapies. Altman explained, "With the newer cell therapies that are seeking approval in the United States, they're all surgical delivery so far. I believe that they've all expressed interest publicly on pursuing different approaches for minimally invasive delivery such as we are pursuing and we could be helpful to them there."
  • James Molloy, Alliance Global Partners: Inquired on enrollment for HF II trial and best practices. Altman shared, "Enrollment numbers right now, we haven't detailed, but we're starting this enrollment slowly because almost all of our clinical team is focused on the efforts, enormous efforts in taking a Phase III trial data set through for regulatory submission."
  • Molloy asked about CMI trial data for EuroPCR in May. Altman said, "the main value of -- we're right now not driving forward aggressively in enrolling the randomized portion of that trial, where we sort of put it on pause to focus on the heart failure program but from a business development perspective, it effectively doubles the market potential of CardiAMP HF."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on regulatory timelines, data significance, trial enrollment, and competitive landscape, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive sentiment, particularly regarding excitement about data and regulatory progress.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, frequently referencing "compelling signals," "remarkably lovely data," and "exciting" prospects, especially regarding regulatory submissions and trial outcomes.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains optimistic but now emphasizes imminent regulatory milestones and more concrete clinical data, while analysts' tone is consistent, seeking clarity and details on timelines and data.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter features more detailed reporting on final Phase III CardiAMP trial data and imminent regulatory submissions, compared to the prior quarter's focus on closing out trials and preparing submissions.
  • Management’s guidance language now anticipates near-term FDA and PMDA interactions, while the previous quarter discussed ongoing preparations.
  • The level of detail on enrollment and trial site activation is now more advanced, with four centers active in CardiAMP HF II.
  • Analysts' questions continue to probe for updates on regulatory progress, trial enrollment, and competitive positioning, but the discussion now centers more on interpreting new trial data and timelines for regulatory decisions.
  • Key financial metrics, such as net loss and cash used in operations, continue to be stable with minor increases, while management’s confidence in cash burn and resource allocation persists.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the cost of closeout activities for the cardiac heart failure trial, ongoing enrollment in CardiAMP HF II, and regulatory activities as drivers of increased R&D expenses.
  • Altman acknowledged the possibility of extensive post-marketing studies involving hundreds of patients and highlighted resource and bandwidth constraints as a factor affecting trial enrollment pace.
  • The competitive landscape is noted, with other cell therapies seeking regulatory approval, though Altman stated, "I'm not concerned about the competitive issues."

Final Takeaway

BioCardia’s leadership underscored a pivotal period with the imminent submission of the CardiAMP system for FDA and PMDA discussion, supported by statistically significant clinical data in key patient subgroups. The company remains focused on advancing its CardiAMP HF II trial, maintaining consistent cash management, and positioning itself in a competitive cell therapy landscape. Management conveyed optimism about upcoming regulatory milestones and multiple near-term catalysts, emphasizing the foundation laid by robust clinical evidence and ongoing engagement with global regulatory agencies.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: California risks fuel crisis unless Middle East war eases, Chevron executive warns
data.description:
California (CVX) may be headed for an energy crisis because of the Iran war, Chevron's (CVX) head of oil refining warned Tuesday, adding that the company may quit refining oil in the state unless officials roll back taxes and regulations.

California is highly exposed to the war disruption rippling across commodity markets because it imports ~20% of its refined fuels from Asia, and shipments from China, South Korea, Singapore, and elsewhere are at risk of slowing significantly, with the effective shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz leaving Asian countries struggling to meet their own domestic demand.

"We have refineries in Asia that are having to cut crude, and so they're going to make less products," Chevron's (CVX) oil refining chief Andy Walz said at the CERAWeek by S&P Global conference in Houston. "What if San Francisco doesn't have the jet fuel it needs? Or Los Angeles? Or maybe gasoline?"

California is disconnected from fuels made in Texas and Louisiana, essentially making it an energy island, which is amplified by multiple refinery closures in the state in recent years due to higher costs caused by regulations designed to fight climate change and restrict oil industry profits.

As a result, California consumers are more exposed than most other Americans to surging energy prices from the Iran war, paying nearly $6/gal for gasoline, compared with a national average near $4/gal.

California's problems are of the state’s own making, Walz said, as "California has decided that they’re going to rely on imports... It's a dangerous game."

New emissions rules proposed by the California Air Resources Board, if implemented, could drive costs for the state's remaining refineries even higher; Chevron (CVX) has estimated the additional expenses could reach $500M within five years.

The state needs to "abandon the tax on refineries or they won't have any refineries in 10 years," Walz said. "If it stays that way, Chevron will be gone in 10 years for sure."

ETFs: (UGA), (CRAK)

data.title: Energy shortages could ​hit Europe by next month, Shell CEO says
data.description:
Europe could feel energy shortages by next month, Shell (SHEL) CEO Wael Sawan warned Tuesday, as the effects of the Middle East war continue to ripple out across global fuel markets, first in South Asia, then Southeast Asia and Northeast Asia, and increasingly in Europe as April approaches.

Countries cannot have national security without energy security, Sawan said at the CERAWeek by S&P Global conference, adding that Shell (SHEL) is trying to work with governments to help them address the energy crisis, including with storage and purchasing.

"We are trying to work with governments to just alert them to the various levers they will need to pull, including on the demand side, including what they need to do around storage," Sawan told the conference.

The war has already affected ​supplies of jet fuel, with diesel set to be next, followed by gasoline as summer driving season ​gets underway in the Northern Hemisphere, the CEO said.

Sawan also said Shell (SHEL) may decide on whether to take final investment decisions on one or two natural gas projects in Venezuela before the end of this year, if allowed by the fiscal and legal situation in the country.

"What we ​are looking at, ⁠at the moment, is where we can add value to Venezuela, the Venezuelan people," the CEO said. "Initially, I would say it's more geared towards gas, and in particular gas that can be monetized through LNG."

data.title: Tela Bio targets at least 8% revenue growth in 2026 while advancing commercial transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TELA Bio, Inc. (TELA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Antony Koblish, Co-founder, CEO & Director, highlighted that 2025, particularly the third and fourth quarters, was a period of "meaningful strategic change across the entire organization." He noted the company executed a significant rebuild of its commercial foundation, resulting in "16% full year growth and achieving record fourth quarter revenues." Koblish stated, "We enter 2026 with the largest, most effective field team in the company's history and the commercial strategy designed to drive durable, predictable growth."
  • Koblish underscored the hiring of high-caliber sales reps, with "40% of the reps having joined TELA in the last 6 months." He clarified this was "an investment in commercial expansion and in recruiting talent" rather than turnover, and added, "We already see the newest reps meaningfully outperform their predecessors in the early stages of their onboarding."
  • Product innovation remains a focus, with Koblish anticipating "additional product launches throughout the year to drive greater share gains in the expansive U.S. market." He also announced the promotion of Dr. Howard Langstein to Chief Medical Officer, effective March 1.
  • European operations were described as "stable, tenured and delivering above plan," with strong OviTex adoption in the U.K. and Netherlands.
  • The company launched OviTex LTR, started the ECHO trial for hiatal hernia, and "reinforced and upsized" its debt facility.
  • Jeffrey Blizard, President, detailed the commercial reorganization, noting the team now comprises "88 quota-carrying territory managers in the U.S.," and that "the team that we need to hit our 2026 targets are largely in place."
  • Roberto Cuca, CFO & COO, stated, "Revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 increased 18% year-over-year to $20.9 million and grew 16% for the full year to $80.3 million." He also noted, "OviTex unit sales grew 20% for the quarter and 22% for the year while PRS unit sales grew 12% for the quarter and for the year."

Outlook

  • Management projects "revenue growth of at least 8% over 2025 with Q1 2026 revenue of approximately $18.5 million."
  • Koblish explained the guidance: "Given the change that we've implemented -- wholesale change, right, across almost every dimension... we thought it would be prudent to set the guidance where we did." He added, "There's so many new reps and new moving parts... we want to make sure that we're giving ourselves that time and flexibility."
  • Cuca described the expected cadence: "We do expect the cadence for the year to be similar to that in prior undisrupted years where you see a step-up from the first to the second quarter, a smaller step up from the second to the third and then a bigger step up again from the third to the fourth quarters."
  • Koblish confirmed ongoing focus on contract execution: "We have to translate that, move it through the hospital processes... and translate that signature now into patients and revenue."

Financial Results

  • Cuca reported, "Revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 increased 18% year-over-year to $20.9 million and grew 16% for the full year to $80.3 million."
  • OviTex revenue grew 12% and OviTex PRS revenue increased 20% for the full year.
  • LIQUIFIX revenue "more than tripled over the fourth quarter of 2024."
  • European sales represented 15% of total revenue, reaching $12.1 million in 2025, a 17% increase over 2024.
  • Gross margin was 66% for Q4 and 68% for the full year.
  • Operating loss was $6.6 million in Q4 and $33.8 million for the year; net loss was $9 million in Q4 and $38.8 million for the year.
  • The company ended 2025 with $50.8 million in cash and cash equivalents.

Q&A

  • Caitlin Cronin, Canaccord Genuity, asked about the "fiscal year top line guidance for at least 8%" and its reduction from Q3's outlook. Koblish responded, "Given the change that we've implemented... it would be prudent to set the guidance where we did... there's a lot of variables, and we wanted to make sure that we're giving ourselves plenty of room."
  • Cronin followed up on contracting progress. Jim Hagen, Senior VP, replied, "2026 is an execution year. We have to translate that, move it through the hospital processes... and translate that signature now into patients and revenue."
  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, asked about Q1 guidance and drivers for a double-digit quarter-over-quarter decline. Koblish cited "smaller territories" and "splitting of territories" as causes, adding, "we are going to concentrate density of reps in smaller areas... so that's going to cause a little bit of loss as we go through these shifts."
  • Takkinen inquired about long-term growth. Koblish said, "We certainly believe that once we get to steady state, we should be back into the double-digit growth or beyond."
  • Michael Sarcone, Jefferies, asked about the reduction in 2026 guidance from "at least 15%" to 8%. Hagen responded, "Our assumptions changed from when we had the Q3 call to now, just appreciating the change curve it takes... is a bit longer and more complex."
  • Matthew O'Brien, Piper Sandler, pressed on the low Q1 revenue guide and the required back-end loaded performance. Hagen cited "volumes in January were low" and added, "impact of the storms on the East Coast... did shuffle some elective procedures."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed skepticism and concern about the significant drop in growth guidance and the impact of organizational changes, with persistent questions around the prudence of the outlook and operational execution.
  • Management's tone was cautious and focused on prudence, frequently emphasizing variables and the need for flexibility, as illustrated by repeated use of the term "prudent" and phrases such as "we want to give ourselves the best chance to do a great job this year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from optimism about acceleration and commercial expansion to a more guarded stance, reflecting uncertainty around the impact of sales force changes and territory realignment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The previous quarter's call outlined a "directional expectation" of at least 15% revenue growth for 2026, compared to the current 8% guidance.
  • Strategic focus shifted from aggressive expansion and hiring to consolidation, execution, and absorption of recent hires.
  • Analyst questions moved from financing and commercial structure to concerns about guidance downgrades and operational risks.
  • Management's confidence moderated, with more emphasis on prudence and the unpredictability of recent organizational changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges cited include the onboarding and productivity ramp of a large cohort of new sales reps, territory realignment, and contract execution complexity.
  • Management noted "contract implementation varies from hospital to hospital" and that "contracting complexity... does affect timing."
  • Weather-related disruptions and lower procedure volumes in January were acknowledged as impacting Q1 performance.
  • Analysts voiced concerns about the ability to achieve back-end loaded growth targets and the timeline for achieving steady-state productivity.

Final Takeaway

TELA Bio management emphasized that 2025 marked a foundational transformation, with the commercial team rebuilt, new leadership established, and a significant investment in sales force expansion. Growth guidance for 2026 is set at a cautious 8% as the company focuses on executing its new commercial model, achieving deeper account penetration, and integrating a wave of new territory managers. Management expressed confidence that the groundwork laid during this organizational shift positions TELA Bio for sustainable long-term growth, even as near-term projections are tempered to account for the transition's inherent variability and complexity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil slides as U.S. reportedly has sent proposal to Iran to end Middle East war
data.description:
U.S. crude oil futures fell nearly 4% to ~$89/bbl after-hours Tuesday following a New York Times report that the Trump administration has sent Iran a 15-point plan to end the Middle East war.

The plan reportedly was delivered via Pakistan, whose army chief has emerged as the key interlocutor between the U.S. and Iran.

The NYT report cited anonymous officials saying the proposal addresses Iran's ballistic missile and nuclear programs, as well as maritime routes; Iran has effectively blocked most Western ships from safely passing through the Strait of Hormuz, which has dramatically reduced the global supply of oil and natural gas, sending their prices soaring.

It is unclear whether Iran would accept the proposal as a basis for negotiations or if Israel, which has been bombing Iran together with the U.S., was on board with the proposal.

President Trump said earlier in the day that the U.S. and Iran were "in negotiations right now" and suggested Iran is eager to make a peace deal, even as the country has denied it is in talks with the U.S.

In Tuesday's regular trading, crude oil futures rebounded from the previous day's selloff as Israel and Iran continued to exchange strikes and Iran targeted other Persian Gulf states.

The Pentagon reportedly plans to deploy the Army's 82nd Airborne Division, with the unit's ~3K soldiers, to support a potential attempt to reopen the Strait of Hormuz or other operations, and U.S. allies in the Persian Gulf are said to be moving toward joining the war against Iran.

The oil market will still demand progress in reopening the Strait of Hormuz, and even then time will be needed to repair oil infrastructure in the region, Ritterbusch & Associates said in a note.

The "vastly overbought" market condition was relieved by Monday's selloff, which left money managers and funds in a position to reload into the long side "should attempts fail by the White House toward diplomacy," Ritterbusch added.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery jumped 4.8% Tuesday to $92.35/bbl, and the front-month Brent contract (CO1:COM) for May climbed 4.5% to $104.49/bbl, the 14th gain in the past 18 sessions for both benchmarks.

U.S. natural gas futures rose after falling along with oil in the previous session, with gains limited by outlooks for warm weather; the front-month Nymex April contract closed up 1.8% to $2.943/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)
